DiagramRecommendation.description: A single recommendation for a diagram to add to a report section.

diagram_type.description: One of the supported DIAGRAM_TYPES keys
diagram_type.title: Diagram Type

description.description: What the diagram should show, grounded in the section content
description.title: Description

DiagramRecommendation.title: DiagramRecommendation

schema_json.description: Full analysis output: which sections deserve diagrams.

recommendations.description: 2-5 sections that would benefit from a visual diagram
recommendations.title: Recommendations

schema_json.title: DiagramAnalysisResult

system:
You are a research report visualization specialist. You identify which report sections benefit most from visual diagrams and select the appropriate diagram type for each.

Available diagram types:
- "process_flow": How X works (step-by-step process)
- "comparison_matrix": X vs Y vs Z comparison
- "causal_chain": A causes B which leads to C
- "hierarchy": Classification/taxonomy of findings
- "timeline": Chronological development of topic
- "pros_cons": Advantages vs disadvantages
- "decision_tree": If X then Y, else Z

Output format example:
{"recommendations": [{"section_id": "s01", "section_title": "Mechanisms of Action", "diagram_type": "process_flow", "description": "Step-by-step process of how compound X binds to receptor Y, triggers pathway Z, and produces effect W as described in the section"}]}
prompt:
Given these report sections, identify 2-5 that would benefit from a visual diagram. For each, specify the diagram type and what the diagram should show.

SECTIONS:
--- Section s01: Intermittent Fasting Protocols and Metabolic Syndrome Definitions (450 words) ---
Intermittent fasting (IF) is a broad umbrella term for dietary patterns in which caloric intake is restricted to defined temporal windows rather than by macronutrient composition. Within the metabolic-syndrome literature, IF has gained popularity as a dietary intervention to improve metabolic health [2], and multiple earlier studies have demonstrated that IF has garnered scientific attention and recognition for its beneficial effects on metabolic outcomes [1]. Among the protocol families represented in the evidence base, the available systematic-review work on alternate-day fasting in adults with overweight or obesity pooled seven trials covering 269 participants aged 18–70 over intervention durations of 4–48 weeks and focused on body weight as the principal endpoint [3].

The evidence synthesized here therefore addresses, in its most developed form, the alternate-day fasting (ADF) variant of the IF family. Other widely discussed protocols — time-restricted eating in its 16:8 and 18:6 variants, the 5:2 pattern, and fasting-mimicking-diet cycles — are not individually anchored in the claim set compiled for this section; rather, the umbrella concept of IF as a metabolic-health intervention is what is directly supported by the retrieved evidence [1][2]. Readers should interpret the subsequent efficacy, safety, and mechanistic discussion against this caveat: the granularity of protocol-level subgroup attribution is downstream of the evidence anchors, and the primary quantitative  [...]

--- Section s02: Pooled Efficacy on Cardiometabolic Endpoints (501 words) ---
Building on the definitional framing of the prior section, the evidence pool assembled here addresses the question of what benefits IF produces and how meaningful those benefits are for cardiometabolic risk. The claim set supports two broad propositions. First, the development of obesity is closely associated with a cluster of metabolic complications — insulin resistance and dyslipidaemia prominent among them — that in turn elevate the risk of developing further cardiometabolic disease [6]. This pathophysiological baseline is what makes caloric-restriction and timing-modification strategies clinically relevant: the upstream dysregulation they target is precisely the one metabolic syndrome is defined by.

Second, several lines of evidence — drawn from both animal and clinical studies — have found that IF-style approaches can produce measurable metabolic improvements, including weight loss and better control of blood glucose [4]. The phrasing of this retrieved evidence is deliberately general ("several studies," "can lead to") and does not at this synthesis level attach specific pooled effect sizes with confidence intervals. Readers interpreting this section should note the qualitative framing: the retrieved claim supports that improvements have been observed, not that a particular effect magnitude has been definitively quantified across all protocols and populations.

Protocol design and individualization appear to modulate the translational viability of these effects. In one  [...]

--- Section s03: Methodological Heterogeneity and GRADE Certainty (506 words) ---
The methodological character of the IF evidence base deserves careful attention before any efficacy or safety conclusion is accepted at face value. The claim set anchoring this section documents three study-design characteristics that together illustrate the heterogeneous architecture of the retrieved literature.

First, the controlled-trial backbone: in one study, 108 adults with metabolic syndrome were randomly sorted into either the time-restricted eating group or the control group [5]. This is a classical randomized two-arm design, and a sample size of 108 is modest by cardiovascular-outcome standards while sufficient to detect moderate intermediate-endpoint effects on glycemic and lipid markers. When pooled in meta-analytic synthesis, trials of this scale contribute to the aggregate effect-size estimates; when considered in isolation, they cannot resolve long-horizon safety or mortality questions.

Second, the real-world observational backbone: separately, real-world adherence and outcome data have been assembled from adult users (ages 18–100 years) of commercial fasting-tracker applications across a multi-year observation window [7]. This methodology — extracting height, weight, and fasting data from consumer-app usage logs — differs fundamentally from supervised RCT protocols. Its strengths are scale (thousands of users across wide age ranges), ecological validity (unsupervised, self-motivated behavior), and the ability to characterize realistic adherence trajectories. [...]

--- Section s04: Long-Term Safety and Observational Evidence (1144 words) ---
Questions about the long-term safety of intermittent fasting sit at the center of ongoing clinical discussion. Short-term supervised trials document favorable intermediate endpoints, but whether those benefits translate into durable health gains — or whether unmeasured risks accumulate over years of adherence — is a question the retrieved evidence base addresses only partially. This section synthesizes what the currently available claims support, distinguishes what they do not yet establish, and articulates the methodological shape of the open questions.

The trial-level backbone for safety-relevant evidence rests on pragmatic randomized controlled trials conducted in community-based adults, comparing time-restricted eating to standard dietary advice with respect to weight, metabolic health, and processed-food consumption [10]. Pragmatic RCT designs are important for the safety discussion because they enroll ecologically representative populations and measure outcomes that bear on real-world translation; their weaknesses are smaller effect sizes than efficacy RCTs and limited power for rare adverse events. Within the retrieved claim set, the pragmatic-RCT anchor is a single descriptive reference rather than a complete quantitative pooled synthesis; readers seeking effect-size precision should interpret this anchor as a design-category exemplar rather than a closed quantitative answer.

The methodological distinction between supervised and free-living intermittent fasting is e [...]

--- Section s05: Biological Mechanisms and Pathways (1071 words) ---
The biological rationale for intermittent fasting's metabolic effects has been extensively discussed in both the popular and scientific literature. The claim set anchoring this section supports a conceptually coherent but deliberately general mechanistic framing; it does not independently substantiate the granular pathway-level claims (circadian clock gene expression, autophagy thresholds, IL-22/ILC3 signaling, adipose beigeing) that appear elsewhere in the IF mechanistic literature. Readers seeking pathway-specific mechanistic precision should therefore treat this section as establishing the conceptual framework rather than fixing the molecular pathway-by-pathway evidence.

The general mechanistic framing begins with what IF actually does behaviorally. With these dietary strategies, the pattern of energy restriction and/or timing of food intake are altered, so that individuals undergo frequently repeated periods of fasting [6]. This description highlights the two degrees of freedom available to any IF protocol: energy-restriction depth (how much is consumed during the eating window) and temporal structure (when the eating window falls within the circadian cycle, and how long the interposed fast is). Any mechanistic account of IF therefore has to address which of these two axes is doing the metabolic work, and whether the two act independently, synergistically, or redundantly.

Recent years have seen growing interest in an eating pattern called time-restricted eating, or inte [...]

--- Section s06: Clinical Implications and Research Priorities (592 words) ---
The clinical implications of the reviewed evidence follow from the particular mixture of sources the retrieved claim set anchors. The anchoring evidence for this section consists of three quite different categories, which together shape what can responsibly be said about patient selection, protocol choice, and next-research priorities.

The systematic-review backbone is provided by a 2017 meta-analysis of short-term intermittent energy restriction interventions for weight management, pooling five trials of 317 adults with overweight or obesity aged 21-69 [3]. This evidence base — short-duration, modest pooled participant count, weight-management endpoint — anchors the short-term efficacy conclusion that IF can produce clinically meaningful weight reduction in the target population. Because the pooled trials are short-duration, the evidence does not itself address durability beyond a few months, and patient counseling based on this anchor should accurately communicate short-horizon applicability rather than extrapolate to year-scale outcomes.

The pragmatic randomized controlled trial evidence is illustrated by a 12-hour TRE vs standard dietary advice comparison in 54 adults with pre-intervention eating durations greater than 14 hours [10]. A pragmatic trial of this size is particularly useful for patient selection because it enrolls a realistically motivated, real-world population and adopts a feasible intervention (a 12-hour window is among the most achievable IF protocols). [...]

Constraints:
- Select at most 5 sections.
- Only recommend a diagram if it adds genuine clarity beyond the text.
- The description must reference specific facts or structures present in the section content.
- Choose the diagram type that best fits the section's analytical structure.
